summary:
UK risk remains elevated, driven primarily by spillovers from the ongoing Middle East conflict (energy and fuel costs) and a continued high baseline of cyber and geopolitical risk. Energy price pressures remain material: Ofgem’s cap fell in April, but Cornwall Insight has warned of a sharp rise from July, and recent fuel-price data point to an immediate consumer impact.

Domestically, the confirmed 7–13 April resident doctors’ strike is a near-term service-disruption risk for the NHS, while severe weather risks have risen in the near term with named storm impacts and multiple active Met Office warnings. No single category currently indicates a national-scale crisis, but several are simultaneously above baseline.

categories.name: Economic / Financial
categories.rationale: Energy-driven cost pressures remain the dominant near-term economic risk. Government and regulator changes have reduced the April price-cap level, but credible forecasts still point to a marked rise from July, and pump prices have risen notably since late February.
drivers: Cornwall Insight forecast an 18% (£288) rise in the July–September Ofgem cap to £1,929 (typical dual-fuel household)
Fuel prices up materially since late February (diesel ~182.8p/l; petrol ~152.0p/l), reinforcing cost-of-living pressure
Brent crude remains elevated versus recent history (late-March level ~£104–105/bbl), implying ongoing inflation sensitivity

categories.name: Military / Security
categories.rationale: The European security environment remains stressed by the Russia-Ukraine war and broader Middle East instability, with continued risk of spillover into energy markets and shipping routes. No new UK-specific kinetic incident was identified in today’s sources.
drivers: Ongoing high-intensity Russia-Ukraine conflict (continuing strikes and positional fighting reported by independent analysts)
Middle East conflict continues to drive second-order impacts (energy/fuel, potential maritime security implications)
UK terrorism threat level remains at a raised baseline in official posture (no change noted)

categories.name: Political / Governance
categories.rationale: Core institutions are functioning, but crisis-management posture is active due to the Middle East conflict and energy-price implications, with COBR convened to coordinate response readiness.
drivers: COBR convened to ensure readiness and coordination for the Iran war impacts
Energy affordability remains politically salient given forecast cap increases
NHS industrial action adds operational pressure and political scrutiny

categories.name: Natural Hazards
categories.rationale: Near-term weather and flood risk is elevated due to named storm impacts (strong winds; snow/blizzard risk in parts of Scotland) alongside multiple active flood alerts and warnings.
drivers: Met Office warnings linked to Storm Dave include disruptive winds (including an amber warning in parts of the UK) and snow/blizzard risk
Environment Agency shows active flood warnings and a larger set of flood alerts (including coastal and groundwater-related)
Combined wind, coastal surge, and lingering groundwater saturation increases local disruption potential

categories.name: Social Stability
categories.rationale: The confirmed 7–13 April resident doctors’ strike is the most concrete near-term driver of disruption risk. Cost pressures from fuel and energy remain a background amplifier of public frustration.
drivers: Resident doctors’ strike confirmed from 7am 7 April to 6.59am 13 April
NHS operational strain risk increases (elective activity disruption; staffing and rota pressure)
Cost-of-living pressures reinforced by fuel-price increases

categories.name: Critical Infrastructure / Cyber
categories.rationale: Cyber risk to UK critical national infrastructure remains elevated, with NCSC urging operators to prepare specifically for highly disruptive or destructive campaigns (including ICS-targeting and data-erasing scenarios).
drivers: NCSC warning highlights the need to prepare for ‘severe’ cyber threats (highly disruptive/destructive attacks)
Recommended defensive measures emphasise patching known vulnerabilities, MFA/access control, and resilience/recovery planning
Threat context includes disruptive malware campaigns observed against energy providers in Europe

indicators.name: Brent crude
indicators.note: Trading Economics data for March 31 showed Brent around $104–105/bbl after a volatile month, leaving UK inflation sensitive to further shocks.

indicators.name: FTSE 100 (GB100)
indicators.note: Trading Economics data for March 31 showed GB100 around 10,214, with the index down ~5% month-on-month but higher year-on-year.

indicators.name: Environment Agency flood status
indicators.note: Active flood warnings and a larger set of flood alerts indicate ongoing local flood risk (notably coastal and groundwater-related).

indicators.name: Met Office severe weather
indicators.note: Named storm warnings include disruptive winds and snow/blizzard risk in parts of the UK.

indicators.name: NHS industrial action
indicators.note: Strike is confirmed from 7am 7 April to 6.59am 13 April, raising near-term disruption risk for planned care.

changes_since_last_update: Overall status reduced from High to Elevated as today’s evidence emphasises elevated but more bounded domestic impacts (weather/flooding and NHS strike) rather than a system-wide acute shock.
Natural Hazards moved up (Guarded → Elevated) due to active Storm Dave warnings and active flood warnings/alerts.
Economic/Financial moved down (High → Elevated) with focus shifting to forecast/second-order energy impacts rather than a fresh acute market dislocation.
Political/Governance moved down (Elevated → Guarded) as governance remains stable despite active crisis coordination (COBR).
priority_watch_items: Storm Dave: monitor escalation from warnings to realised disruption (power outages, transport disruption, coastal flooding).
Flooding: watch any increase in Environment Agency flood warnings (expected flooding) and whether groundwater alerts persist/expand.
Resident doctors’ strike (7–13 April): monitor NHS service impacts, any local critical-incident declarations, and knock-on effects to urgent care.
Energy affordability: monitor the July Ofgem cap outcome versus Cornwall Insight forecasts and associated policy response.
CNI cyber posture: track NCSC advisories and any UK-reported disruptive ransomware/ICS incidents affecting essential services.
source_notes: Weather hazard evidence based on Met Office UK warnings page (Storm Dave; amber and yellow warnings) and the Environment Agency flood alerts/warnings service (counts and locations).
Energy/fuel pressure evidence based on ITV reporting of COBR convening and quantified Ofgem cap and pump-price impacts.
Strike timing evidence based on GOV.UK DHSC media blog confirming resident doctors industrial action dates/times.
Cyber posture evidence based on Infosecurity Magazine reporting on NCSC guidance around ‘severe’ cyber threats and recommended mitigations.
Market indicators use publicly available reference sites (Trading Economics for Brent/GB100 levels and Wise for indicative GBP/USD); figures are indicative and may lag intraday moves.
